paragraphs.body: How To Walk on Broken Glass

annotations.comment: I confess that this title launched Annie Lennox’s song “Walking on Broken Glass” in my head. You may or may not want that effect (which might be exclusive to Gen X and older).

paragraphs.body: Google tells me it’s possible to walk on broken glass, which is good because there is no Plan B. Walking on glass is easier than it looks, according to the man on my computer, like most things in life. The man’s name is XTremeCircusSurvivalTips, and he starts the video with some advice. “Don’t fuck up,” he tells me, the middle word, fuck, bleeped out by an aggressive honking noise, “and don’t forget to like and subscribe.”

annotations.comment: Since this refers to a specific video, maybe that title goes here, not Google?
I very much enjoyed “the man’s name is…”

paragraphs.body: There’s a trick to it apparently. XTreme explains it well, through countless camera cuts and musical stings. People think that the glass is fake or plastic, but it’s glass. It’s real, and it can hurt you just as bad as any other glass. It is entirely possible to cut yourself or even die by walking on glass if you fuck it up. That’s why the key is to not fuck it up.

annotations.text: People think that the glass is fake or plastic, but it’s glass. It’s real, and it can hurt you just as bad as any other glass
annotations.comment: This paragraph started out being specifically about the video, but then it suddenly broadens to all glass, or at least felt that way?

paragraphs.body: To walk on broken glass, I’ll first have to prepare the glass. Fresher glass is sharper, so due to my beginner status I’m supposed to put broken glass in a towel-wrapped-tupperware and then put that tupperware into the dryer for an hour or so. This will smooth down the glass and, depending on how long the glass is in the dryer, may even give it a sea glass level of safety and ease. Once I get used to this easier glass, I may eventually move on to fresh glass. Even fresh glass should be mixed before actually stepping on it, as this grinds some of the sharper bits to sand, making it safer. It can still cut, but this makes injury or cuts less likely. To do this, performers typically mix the glass to demonstrate that it is real, building the sensory experience, shifting away bad pieces. I have never considered myself a performer, but XTreme tells me that everyone is, that it’s buried deep in my alpha instincts, right next to my balls. I begin to wonder if I am truly the intended audience for this video. It seems walking on glass may be a rather male dominated field. But I won’t give up.

annotations.comment: Are we still following the video here? Also, I wanted the word “barefoot” in here at some point.
What? Fascinating.
annotations.context_left: osed to put broken glass in a towel-wrapped-tupperware and then put that tupperware into the dryer f
annotations.context_right: an hour or so. This will smooth down the glass and, depending on how long the glass is in the dryer

annotations.comment: This is almost surely a Shannon-only thing, but I was confused about how the towel would stay on the Tupperware in a dryer.
I started to find the narrator funny here.
annotations.context_left: , I’ll first have to prepare the glass. Fresher glass is sharper, so due to my beginner status I’m s
annotations.context_right: posed to put broken glass in a towel-wrapped-tupperware and then put that tupperware into the dryer

paragraphs.body: There’s a hair salon, four or five blocks down from my apartment, named Styles of Aeschylus, but more importantly there is a hairstylist named Barbara. Barbara is a beautiful woman, don’t let the name fool you. Square jawed, and hooked nosed, with sturdy arms that make her look more destined from rugby than cosmetology. But of course, she defies expectations. Her eyes, a beautiful brown, her hair an enticing and forever shifting rainbow of new dyes and styles, last week chartreuse, today a brilliant pink that makes me forget my undying hatred for the color. She raises her scissors, and they glisten in the light, like the swipe of glitter on her cheek that I would give my life to lick. She leans in close. If I focus, I can almost feel her breath on my face as she brings the scissors to my bangs and begins to cut.

annotations.comment: I absolutely did not believe this. (But it’s funny anyway)
Wording is off. “Barbara there.” Maybe?
annotations.context_left: own from my apartment, named Styles of Aeschylus, but more importantly there is a hairstylist named
annotations.context_right: . Barbara is a beautiful woman, don’t let the name fool you. Square jawed, and hooked nosed, with st

annotations.comment: This didn’t sound beautiful to me, so I appreciated the narrator’s sense that beauty can be fairly wide-ranging.

paragraphs.body: She is a talented woman, and perhaps that is her most attractive quality, if I could ever sum it up so succinctly. Her talents are myriad, ranging from latte art to rolla bolla. She has strong feelings about her older sister, although I don’t know which ones. She owns a tarantula.

annotations.comment: I find myself torn here. I was enjoying reading about the glass, and feel sad that I’ve been moved along without any connection to this new section. This made me somewhat resentful about learning about Barbara. But that aside, this character description goes on a bit long for not knowing what her role in the story (and the narrator’s life) is.

paragraphs.body: This information has not come easily. Barbara is a gruff woman of very few words. Most questions are answered with noncommittal shrugs and the occasional “sure.” Talking to her requires a careful hand, a willingness to pry. A willingness to suffer five, ten, fifteen crappy haircuts. As many as it takes. I’m on number thirty-two. All this information I have gathered up, a crumb at a time, into my cupped palms. I keep it in a journal, and in the notes app on my phone.

annotations.text: in a journal, and in the notes app
annotations.comment: This information is important enough that the narrator has noted it down twice?

paragraphs.body: Hair is not her true passion, as is clear from the rather inconsistent and jagged quality of her cuts, but it supports her true infatuation: burlesque. In a week, she will be doing a burlesque show dressed as Dionysus, which doesn’t matter. That’s the boring bit. What makes this show special is that she will perform while walking upon a field of broken glass. She does not know how yet, but she’ll learn. This is my chance. This is why I need Xtreme.

annotations.comment: Showing a little of this dialogue might be helpful. I was very glad to finally see the connection! Also — why does Dionysus need to walk on broken glass?

paragraphs.body: I have a friend who drinks, has since well before I met her freshman year. One night I watched her shake, held her head in my lap as she threw up on me five times. I had vomit in my hair, a chunk I did not notice until the waiter pointed it out to me on a brunch date the next morning. She owes me one, and she knows it.

annotations.comment: Gross. Might be funny to let us know who the narrator was at brunch with.

paragraphs.body: The phone rings three times before she picks up, answering with a grunt instead of a greeting. I say hello, ask her how’s she doing.
“Well right now I’m shitting.”

She doesn’t have to pick up the phone on the toilet, and I tell her that. She thought it was an emergency, which it isn’t technically, but when I offer to call back in a few minutes she tells me not to. She recently broke up with her boyfriend, and it’s clear she’s lonely, life spiraling away from the anticipated plan– no job, no college. What will this new future look like for her? We don’t ask. Instead, we shoot the shit for a while, gossiping about the dramatic downfalls of various friends and acquaintances.

annotations.text: toilet, and I tell her that.
annotations.comment: I concur, this friend is gross.

While this is nicely written, I think it might be beneficial to the pacing of the story to get on to the glass walking portion of the conversation. (But that said, you’re good with dialogue so how about some with Barbara?)

paragraphs.body: She tells me the cops showed up at Georgie’s house. He’d been stealing stop signs, license plates caught on the security David footage. They pulled up, warrant in hand, took a look around. Found a piece under his bed, a couple bags of stupidity in his drawer. His dad tried to take the fall, now they’re both screwed.
There goes Kent State.
“Bad solutions,” we say, shaking our heads and waggling our fingers. We would never do anything like that.

annotations.text: shaking our heads and waggling our fingers.
annotations.comment: No clue what his looks like. Again, pretty far wandered away.

paragraphs.body: I tell her about the sign I stole from the basketball stadium of my university, when my friend helped me break in after hours. She tells me about six dates she went on with a man who kept calling her Lucy. She says he’s pretty sweet, maybe too sweet, but she’s seeing him again tomorrow just in case.

I don’t tell her about the lump in my dad’s armpit, the one we’re trying not to think about, not after last year’s near disaster. I don’t tell her about the dog. I’m sure she has plenty of things she wants to tell me, things that aren’t all up in Georgie’s business, but she doesn’t either. We’re not that kind of friend anymore, two years and 467 long miles of highway stretched taut between us like an overpulled rubber band. Instead, I tell her to send me some bottles. I tell her I will pay for the shipping. I don’t tell her I’m in love.

annotations.text: , I tell her to send me some bottles.
annotations.comment: I need more here — what’s in the bottles. I couldn’t unpack the deal with the name — the friend being called Lucy, learning that the narrator goes by Lucy, Barbara not saying Lucy. ?
annotations.context_right: I tell her I will pa

paragraphs.body: Barbara doesn’t call me Lucy. I haven’t heard her say my name yet, but I know she will.

The bottles arrive just, expedited at great expense, but this needs to be perfect, so it doesn’t matter. I’m new to grand gestures, dated a couple bland guys in high school, but they weren’t much to write home about. No one, man or woman, could hold a candle to Barbara. It would be like holding a candle to a wildfire.

The girls in my dorm don’t appreciate my slightly unconventional use of our laundry room. I receive several texts, pointedly sent to the group chat, requesting that somebody please stop using a hammer to shatter drinking vessels in communal spaces. Some people have shit to do. I don’t see these texts, too consumed by my task, until my towel-wrapped tupperware of glass has been lovingly placed into the dryer with a gentle pat to the lid. Bunch of stiffs.

annotations.comment: This made me laugh. But I did wonder why these bottles are so special?

paragraphs.body: I text back a quick whoops, and a sweaty emoji, then Do Not Disturb my phone. I need to focus. I watch the tupperware circle through the dryer's round little window. With each rotation, the thump of the tupperware is offset, almost beautifully, by the clattering crash of hundreds of glass chunks and shards breaking and molding to my needs. I can’t see the glass, in all its sparkling glory, but each thump clattercrash brings me closer to Barbara. Thump clattercrash. Thump clatterclass. Like the pounding of my heart, again, and again, and again.

annotations.comment: I love this wording. It’s very George Saunders. I also want to point out that this narrator seems like a giant pain in the ass in many ways. That’s not bad or wrong, just noting.

paragraphs.body: It takes fifteen minutes for the resident director, Sophie, to stop by. She informs me, in that cheerful way of hers, that several students have made complaints about the noise. They’re growing concerned.
“It looks like you might need a wellness catchup,” peps Sophie.

annotations.comment: The super sunny RA feels a little like a trope in a story that has mostly avoided stereotypes.

paragraphs.body: I don’t want a catchup, so I tell her I prefer mustard. She doesn’t laugh, annoyance poking through as she has me remove my tupperware, yes even the glass, from the communal laundry room. Barbara would think I was funny. She would never treat me like this. I try to explain, tell her that I need this glass smooth, and it won’t be smooth yet. I try to make it clear that I am running out of time, that I need this done by my appointment tonight, but she doesn’t know Barbara, so she doesn’t get it.

annotations.text: Barbara would think I was funny.
annotations.comment: I didn’t really get the sense that Barbara has a great sense of humor, so it was interesting to read this. I couldn’t decide if the narrator was correct or aspirational.

This sense of urgency might appear in the story earlier? I also wanted to understand the plan here a bit more — to teach Barbara to do this? TO give Barbara the glass? (Did Barbara at some point indicate she’d be walking on glass incorrectly?)

paragraphs.body: “I don’t think this is your best idea,” she says. “How about we talk it through?”

annotations.comment: Sort of love this RA.

paragraphs.body: I don’t. My mind’s made up. If the glass isn’t ready, it isn’t ready. I’ll just be more ready. I am Alexander, Napoleon. I am Cesar. I will not let haters shroud my mind with doubt.

Styles of Aeschylus sits between a small lecture hall and a blood bank. Seamlessly incorporated into campus, it sees a variety of students, most too new to the city to know that there are better and cheaper places to get a mediocre fade. Sometimes, the hairstylists compete to see who can get a client to believe the most outrageous lie. One of James’s clients thinks he has 42 different brothers. Katherine’s think she is dying of a variety of different ailments, each stacked on top of the other like little cartoon kids in a trench coat. Katherine always gets the best tips.

annotations.comment: Ha. I really wanted to *see* and *hear* this salon. It’s great but I can’t quite picture it.

paragraphs.body: The hairstylists are not new to the city, but they don’t plan to stop here either. The dirty blue walls of this fine establishment, the weird smell and stains in the public bathroom, they’re just a steppingstone on the journey to Bigger and Better. When they write memoirs, they’ll skip over this place entirely.

annotations.text: The hairstylists are not new to the city, but they don’t plan to stop here either. The dirty blue walls of this fine establishment, the weird smell and stains in the public bathroom, they’re just a steppingstone on the journey to Bigger and Better. When they write memoirs, they’ll skip over this place entirely.
annotations.comment: This paragraph doesn’t do much for you. Slows down the story as it is supposed to pick up steam.

paragraphs.body: The girl walks in with a large object wrapped in a neon green buzz lightyear towel. She’s one of Barbara’s clients, a loyal one but not particularly memorable. They don’t really remember her name, but then, why would they?

annotations.text: one of Barbara’s clients, a loyal one but not particularly memorable
annotations.comment: How does the narrator know this?

paragraphs.body: The hairstylists give her little notice until she unwraps the towel, tossing it sideways into a pile of hair clippings. Brown, red, and golden snippets, just finally sweeped, scatter to every corner of the store. She opens the container pouring shattered glass to the floor in a cacophony of noise.
“This is for Barbara,” she says, making eye contact from across the store.

“I love you,” she says, mixing the glass with her bare hand. Even in the low light, the glass shines, sharp and beautiful. The shifting makes a nearly unbearable noise on the linoleum, even over the sounds of Jens clippers, still going, forgotten, in her left hand. No one can look away. No one even dares to breathe.

annotations.comment: Great, love this. I confess that I’ve lost the thread of why the narrator is doing this, exactly? It seems to not be about Barbara’s burlesque anymore?

paragraphs.body: The girl hovers her foot over the glass.
And then she takes a step.

The scream seems to come from outside of the girl, but it doesn’t, rushing out of her as if yanked as piece after piece of sharp glass slashes its way into the soles of her feet. Air rushes up her throat, the vestibular folds in her larynx vibrating and clapping, spasming under the weight of pain. There is no pitch, there is no song. Nothing beautiful is happening here today.

annotations.comment: I kinda inwardly screamed too.

annotations.text: Air rushes up her throat, the vestibular folds in her larynx vibrating and clapping, spasming under the weight of pain.
annotations.comment: Probably an unpopular opinion but this took me out of the story.

paragraphs.body: There’s a rush of movement as the girl falls backwards, landing atop the glass.  Client makes a sound like a dog choking up grass. James and Katherine rush to catch the girl by the arms. Jen frantically phones an ambulance. The girl is crying now, a pathetic, mournful sound that the hairstylists can feel in their bones.
Barbara, frozen, watches the world rush together as it falls apart.

annotations.comment: The ending wasn’t as strong as it could be for me because the plot was a little misaligned in the story. It seemed like Barbara was the one who needed to learn to walk on glass and I thought perhps the narrator/girl was going to teach her —but by the end, the girl is confessing her love for Barbara with the broken glass, for reasons I didn’t quite follow. That confusion over the flow of the plot made it hard to make the ending work for me. All of that said, there is so much here I like, Joseph! Your characterization, the specificity of the salon and laundry room, the little borrowed bits of college life and, of course, the stupidity of an intense crush are all really great. I think you’ll have a fantastic story here, if you can step back and think through the conflict and plot. I’m very happy to have a conversation with you about both, but I would start from: what does your main character want, what will she/he/they do to get it and (possibly most importantly here) who or what is keeping them from achieving that want? Great work! Happy revising.
